title: The Ward: Toronto's First Immigrant Neighbourhood
description: How a dense downtown neighbourhood became the landing pad for waves of newcomers who shaped the city's identity.
transcript: In the heart of what is now Nathan Phillips Square stood one of Toronto's most storied neighbourhoods. The Ward, as it was known, was home to successive waves of immigrants from the 1840s onward. Irish, Jewish, Italian, and Chinese communities each left their mark on these dense city blocks before urban renewal swept them away in the 1950s and 60s.

entities.name: The Ward
entities.description: Historic immigrant neighbourhood demolished for Nathan Phillips Square and City Hall

entities.name: Nathan Phillips Square
entities.description: Toronto's main civic square, built 1965 on the site of The Ward

entities.name: Urban Renewal
entities.description: Post-war policy of demolishing 'slum' neighbourhoods for modernist redevelopment

title: Kensington Market: A Living Museum
description: From Jewish market stalls to global food destination, Kensington Market's evolution mirrors Toronto's changing demographics.
transcript: Walk through Kensington Market today and you're walking through layers of Toronto's history. In the 1920s, these were Jewish market stalls selling chickens and fish. By the 1960s, Portuguese bakeries appeared. Then came Caribbean spice shops, Vietnamese pho houses, and Mexican taquerias. The market has never been frozen in time. It evolves with whoever calls Toronto home.

entities.name: Kensington Market
entities.description: Bohemian neighbourhood and open-air market in downtown Toronto, designated a National Historic Site in 2006

entities.name: Jewish Toronto
entities.description: The Jewish community's deep roots in Toronto, from Spadina Avenue garment factories to present day

entities.name: Portuguese Community
entities.description: Portuguese immigration to Toronto peaked in the 1950s-70s, centred around Dundas and Ossington

title: The Jamaican Patty: Toronto's Signature Bite
description: How the Jamaican patty became a Toronto staple, from Caribbean kitchens to corner shops across the city.
transcript: In the 1970s and 80s, Eglinton Avenue West between Oakwood and Dufferin was the centre of Caribbean life in Toronto. Record shops, patty shops, barbershops, and sound system dances defined the strip. Artists like Leroy Sibbles and Jackie Mittoo recorded at studios nearby. In 2015, the city officially designated a stretch of Eglinton as Reggae Lane, but by then gentrification and the Crosstown LRT construction had already transformed the neighbourhood.

entities.name: Little Jamaica
entities.description: Toronto neighbourhood along Eglinton West, historic centre of Jamaican-Canadian community

entities.name: Leroy Sibbles
entities.description: Jamaican-Canadian reggae musician, founding member of The Heptones, Toronto resident since the 1970s

entities.name: Crosstown LRT
entities.description: Major transit construction project along Eglinton Avenue, disrupting businesses for over a decade

title: The Don Valley: Toronto's Hidden Wilderness
description: The ravine system that runs through Toronto's east end holds stories of industry, ecology, and reinvention.
transcript: Before the Don Valley Parkway carved through it, the Don River valley was Toronto's industrial backbone. Brickyards, tanneries, and slaughterhouses lined the river. By the 1960s, it was one of the most polluted waterways in Canada. Today, the Don Valley trails are some of the most popular green spaces in the city, and the Evergreen Brick Works has transformed a former quarry into a community hub for sustainability.

entities.name: Don Valley
entities.description: Major ravine system cutting through Toronto's east side, from the Oak Ridges Moraine to the harbour

entities.name: Evergreen Brick Works
entities.description: Former Don Valley Pressed Brick Works, now a community environmental centre

entities.name: Don River Restoration
entities.description: Ongoing effort to naturalize the Don River mouth and restore ecological health

title: Toronto's First NBA Team: The Raptors Story
description: How Toronto got its first NBA franchise and what it meant for the city's identity on the world stage.
transcript: In 1967, as Canada celebrated its centennial, Toronto's Caribbean community created its own celebration. What started as a modest parade along University Avenue has grown into one of North America's largest street festivals, drawing over a million visitors each summer. The mas bands, the steel pans, the soca trucks shaking Lakeshore Boulevard, these are sounds and sights that belong to Toronto as much as they belong to the islands.

entities.name: Toronto Caribbean Carnival
entities.description: Annual Caribbean cultural festival, formerly known as Caribana, held since 1967

entities.name: Canada's Centennial
entities.description: 1967 celebration of Canada's 100th anniversary of Confederation

entities.name: Lakeshore Boulevard
entities.description: Major waterfront road, traditional parade route for the carnival

title: Jane Jacobs and the Spadina Expressway Fight
description: How citizen activism stopped a highway from destroying downtown Toronto's neighbourhoods.
transcript: In 1969, the Ontario government planned to extend the Spadina Expressway straight through the heart of downtown Toronto. It would have leveled blocks of homes in The Annex, Kensington Market, and Chinatown. Jane Jacobs, who had recently moved to Toronto from New York, joined a coalition of residents who fought the highway. Their campaign, Stop Spadina Save Our City, succeeded in 1971 when Premier Bill Davis cancelled the project. That decision shaped modern Toronto more than any building ever built.

entities.name: Jane Jacobs
entities.description: Urban theorist and activist, author of The Death and Life of Great American Cities, Toronto resident from 1968 until her death in 2006

entities.name: Spadina Expressway
entities.description: Proposed highway that would have cut through downtown Toronto, cancelled in 1971 after citizen opposition

entities.name: Bill Davis
entities.description: Premier of Ontario (1971-1985) who cancelled the Spadina Expressway

title: Regent Park: Demolition and Rebirth
description: Canada's first and largest social housing project faces demolition and redevelopment, displacing thousands.
transcript: Built in the late 1940s, Regent Park was supposed to be the solution to Toronto's housing crisis. For decades it was Canada's largest social housing project, home to thousands of families from around the world. By the 2000s, the buildings were deteriorating and the neighbourhood was isolated from the surrounding city. The revitalization plan promised modern mixed-income housing, but for many long-time residents, it meant displacement from the only community they had ever known.

entities.name: Regent Park
entities.description: Canada's oldest and largest social housing project, built 1948, undergoing revitalization since 2005

entities.name: Toronto Community Housing
entities.description: Largest social housing provider in Canada, managing over 58,000 units

entities.name: Regent Park Revitalization
entities.description: Multi-phase redevelopment replacing original buildings with mixed-income communities

title: The Toronto Islands: A Community Under Threat
description: The residents of Ward's Island and Algonquin Island fought for decades to keep their homes.
transcript: Just a ten-minute ferry ride from downtown sits one of Toronto's most unusual communities. The Toronto Islands have been home to a small residential community since the 1800s. In the 1950s, Metro Toronto began demolishing homes to create parkland. Residents fought back, and after decades of legal battles, the Island community won the right to stay through a 99-year land lease in 1993. Today, about 600 people live on the islands, a car-free village within sight of the skyline.

entities.name: Toronto Islands
entities.description: Chain of small islands in Toronto harbour, home to a residential community and public parkland

entities.name: Ward's Island
entities.description: Easternmost of the Toronto Islands, residential community since the 1880s

entities.name: Island Community Land Trust
entities.description: 1993 agreement granting island residents a 99-year lease on their homes

title: Honest Ed's: The End of an Era
description: When Toronto's most beloved discount store closed, it took a piece of the city's soul with it.
transcript: For 64 years, Honest Ed's was a Toronto landmark. Ed Mirvish opened his bargain store at the corner of Bloor and Bathurst in 1948, and it became the place where new immigrants outfitted their first Canadian apartments. The store's hand-painted signs and overwhelming chaos were part of its charm. When it closed in 2016 to make way for condominiums, thousands lined up for one last visit. The neon signs were saved, but the spirit of the place, the feeling of abundance for everyone, that was harder to preserve.

entities.name: Honest Ed's
entities.description: Legendary discount department store at Bloor and Bathurst, operated 1948-2016

entities.name: Ed Mirvish
entities.description: Toronto businessman, philanthropist, and theatre impresario, founder of Honest Ed's and restorer of the Royal Alexandra Theatre

entities.name: Mirvish Village Redevelopment
entities.description: Mixed-use development replacing Honest Ed's and surrounding properties, completed 2023

title: The Waterfront: Reclaiming the Lake
description: Toronto spent a century turning its back on Lake Ontario. Now it's trying to turn around.
transcript: For most of the 20th century, Toronto's waterfront was a barrier of railways, highways, and industrial land separating the city from its lake. The Gardiner Expressway, completed in 1966, became the defining symbol of that separation. Since 2001, Waterfront Toronto has been working to reconnect the city with Lake Ontario. Projects like the Bentway, which transformed the space under the Gardiner into a public park, show what is possible. But the full vision, a continuous public waterfront from the Humber River to the Scarborough Bluffs, remains decades away.

entities.name: Gardiner Expressway
entities.description: Elevated highway along Toronto's waterfront, completed 1966, subject of ongoing demolition debates

entities.name: Waterfront Toronto
entities.description: Government agency created in 2001 to oversee waterfront revitalization

entities.name: The Bentway
entities.description: Public space created under the Gardiner Expressway, opened 2018, featuring skating trail, gardens, and performance space
